title: Terms implied into a consumer contract by the Consumer Rights Act 2015
summary: 9084 Contract — CRA 2015 quality, fitness, description, and digital content standards.

sections.content: **CRA 2015 implied terms** (3.2.2) — know **statutory standards** and **consumer remedies**.
The Scope and Application of the Consumer Rights Act 2015

The Consumer Rights Act 2015 (CRA) was enacted to consolidate, simplify, and strengthen UK consumer law. It applies to contracts made between a 'trader' and a 'consumer' on or after 1 October 2015. A 'consumer' is defined in s.2(3) as 'an individual acting for purposes that are wholly or mainly outside that individual’s trade, business, craft or profession'. Conversely, a 'trader' (s.2(2)) is a person acting for purposes relating to their trade, business, craft or profession. This distinction is crucial; the Act's protections do not apply to business-to-business (B2B) or consumer-to-consumer (C2C) contracts. The CRA 2015 implies key terms into contracts for goods, services, and, for the first time under specific legislation, digital content, ensuring consumers receive a baseline level of protection.

items: Applies to Business-to-Consumer (B2C) contracts only.
Defines 'consumer' as an individual acting outside their business capacity.
Defines 'trader' as a person acting within their business capacity.
Consolidates consumer protection from previous legislation like the Sale of Goods Act 1979 and Supply of Goods and Services Act 1982.
Covers contracts for goods, services, and digital content.

sections.content: Section 9: Goods to be of Satisfactory Quality

Section 9 of the CRA 2015 implies a term that all goods supplied must be of satisfactory quality. This objective standard is assessed by what a 'reasonable person' would regard as satisfactory, considering the price, description, and all other relevant circumstances. Section 9(3) provides a non-exhaustive list of factors to assess quality, including fitness for all common purposes, appearance and finish, freedom from minor defects, safety, and durability. The standard is flexible. For example, in Rogers v Parish (1987), a new £16,000 Range Rover had minor defects in the engine, gearbox, and bodywork. The court held it was not of satisfactory quality; a consumer is entitled to expect a new, premium car to be free from even minor defects. This contrasts with Bramhill v Edwards (2004), where a motorhome slightly wider than the legal limit was deemed satisfactory as the defect was minor and did not affect use or safety.

items: The standard is that of a 'reasonable person'.
Price and description are key factors in setting expectations of quality.
Aspects of quality include: fitness for common purposes, appearance, freedom from minor defects, safety, and durability.
This term cannot be invoked for defects specifically drawn to the consumer's attention before the contract was made.
It also does not apply to defects that should have been apparent on a reasonable examination by the consumer (if they examined the goods).

sections.content: Section 10: Fitness for a Particular Purpose

Section 10 implies a term that goods will be reasonably fit for any particular purpose that the consumer makes known to the trader. This protection is triggered when the consumer, either expressly or by implication, communicates a specific use for the item which may be different from its common purpose. Crucially, the consumer must be relying on the trader's skill or judgement. For instance, if a consumer tells a hardware shop assistant they need adhesive to stick plastic to metal, the adhesive sold must be fit for that specific purpose. Similarly, if a buyer tells a computer store they need software for video editing on a Mac, and they are sold a Windows-only version, this would be a breach of s.10. The principle was established in pre-CRA cases like Griffiths v Peter Conway Ltd (1939), where a man with abnormally sensitive skin could not claim a coat was unfit, as he had not made his specific sensitivity known to the seller.

items: The consumer must make their particular purpose known to the trader, either expressly or by implication.
The consumer must demonstrate reliance on the trader's skill or judgement.
This term applies even if the purpose is not a common one for the goods.
If the consumer does not rely, or it is unreasonable for them to rely, on the trader's skill, s.10 will not apply.

sections.content: Section 11: Goods to be as Described

Under s.11 of the CRA 2015, where goods are supplied by description, there is an implied term that the goods will correspond with that description. This is a strict liability term, meaning the trader is in breach even if the goods are of satisfactory quality, if they do not match the description given. The description can be written (e.g., on packaging or a website), pictorial, or verbal. The case of Re Moore & Co and Landauer & Co (1921), although pre-CRA, illustrates the strictness; tins of fruit were delivered in cases of 24 instead of the described 30, and despite the total quantity being correct, this was a breach. This strict approach was also seen in Arcos Ltd v E A Ronaasen & Son (1933), where wooden staves that were a fraction of an inch thicker than described were held to be a breach, even though they were still perfectly usable for their intended purpose of making barrels. Under the CRA, every part of the description is a contractual term.

items: This is a strict liability term; fault is not required.
The description can be written, verbal, or pictorial.
Goods must match the description, even if they are of satisfactory quality.
This term also applies where the consumer sees the goods but relies on the description, for example, in a catalogue or online.

sections.content: Consumer Remedies for Goods (ss.19-24)

The CRA 2015 provides a clear, tiered system of remedies if goods do not conform to the contract by breaching one of the implied terms (ss. 9, 10, 11). This structure is a key departure from the older Sale of Goods Act regime.
Tier 1: The Short-Term Right to Reject (ss.20-22)

For a period of 30 days from the date of delivery (or ownership transfer), the consumer has the right to reject the goods and claim a full refund. The consumer must indicate to the trader that they are rejecting the goods. The trader must provide the refund without undue delay and within 14 days, and must bear any reasonable costs of returning the goods.
Tier 2: The Right to Repair or Replacement (s.23)

If the 30-day period has passed, or if the consumer chooses not to reject the goods, they have the right to request a repair or a replacement. The trader must carry this out within a reasonable time, without causing significant inconvenience to the consumer, and at the trader's own expense. The consumer cannot demand both, and the trader can refuse one of the options if it is impossible or disproportionately expensive compared to the other.
Tier 3: Price Reduction or the Final Right to Reject (s.24)

The consumer becomes entitled to a second-tier remedy if: (a) a repair or replacement is impossible or has failed; (b) the trader has not performed the repair/replacement within a reasonable time or without significant inconvenience. The consumer can then choose either: 
- **A price reduction:** An 'appropriate amount' is refunded to the consumer, who keeps the goods. This could be up to 100% of the price.
- **The final right to reject:** The consumer rejects the goods for a refund. However, if this right is exercised more than 6 months after delivery, the trader is entitled to make a deduction from the refund to account for the use the consumer has had of the goods (this does not apply to motor vehicles).
Statutory Rights for Digital Content (ss.34-36)

A significant development in the CRA 2015 was the introduction of specific statutory rights for 'digital content'. This is defined as 'data which are produced and supplied in digital form', such as software, apps, games, e-books, and streamed media. Previously, the legal status of such content was unclear. The CRA 2015 implies terms for digital content that mirror those for goods. Section 34 requires digital content to be of satisfactory quality. Section 35 requires it to be fit for a particular purpose made known by the consumer. Section 36 requires it to be as described by the trader. These provisions provide consumers with clear rights and remedies, such as the right to repair or replacement, if their digital purchases are faulty.

items: First UK legislation to provide specific consumer rights for digital content.
Implied terms mirror those for goods: satisfactory quality (s.34), fitness for purpose (s.35), and as described (s.36).
Quality includes being free from viruses and minor bugs that affect functionality.
Remedies for digital content are similar but distinct: right to repair/replacement, then a right to a price reduction (s.42-44). There is no 'short-term right to reject'.

sections.content: In problem questions, always identify the parties first to establish if it is a B2C contract, thus bringing the CRA 2015 into play. Then, methodically apply the relevant sections (s.9, s.10, s.11) to the facts. For example, if a laptop is slow, it's a s.9 'satisfactory quality' issue. If the consumer said they needed it for high-end video editing and it can't cope, it's a s.10 'fitness for purpose' issue. If it was advertised with 16GB of RAM but only has 8GB, it's a s.11 'as described' issue. Stating the specific section number demonstrates precise legal knowledge.

sections.question: Maya buys a laptop online from TechCo for £600, described as 'brand new, 8GB RAM'. On delivery the laptop has 4GB RAM and scratches. Maya rejects it within 10 days. Advise Maya's remedies under CRA 2015.
sections.solution:
**Consumer contract:** Maya is a **consumer**; TechCo a **trader** — **CRA 2015** applies.

**Implied terms breached:**
- **As described** (s11) — 4GB not 8GB.
- **Satisfactory quality** (s9) — scratches on 'new' laptop.
- **Fit for purpose** (s10) — if Maya specified use requiring 8GB.

**Non-conforming goods:** Goods **do not conform** to contract (s19).

**Remedy — short-term right to reject (s20–22):** Within **30 days**, Maya may **reject** and demand **full refund** — no obligation to accept repair first.

**Trader's duty:** TechCo must **refund without undue delay** and within 14 days of receiving goods back.

**Conclusion:** Maya entitled to **reject and full refund** — express description and implied quality terms both breached.

sections.question: Ben buys a washing machine from an appliance store for £500. After four months, it develops a serious leak. The store sends an engineer who attempts a repair, but the leak persists. The store cannot offer a replacement as the model is discontinued. Ben wants to keep the machine but get some money back. An independent expert values the faulty machine at £150. Advise Ben on his rights and calculate the potential refund.
sections.solution:
**1. Identify Contract and Breach:**
- This is a B2C contract, so the CRA 2015 applies.
- The machine is not of 'satisfactory quality' (s.9) as it is not durable and leaks after only four months.

**2. Determine the Remedy Path:**
- The 30-day 'short-term right to reject' has expired.
- Ben is entitled to the 'first-tier' remedy: a repair or replacement (s.23).
- The trader attempted one repair, which failed. Replacement is not possible.

**3. Access the 'Second-Tier' Remedy:**
- As the first-tier remedy has failed, Ben can now choose a 'second-tier' remedy under s.24: a price reduction or the final right to reject.
- Ben chooses to keep the item and get a price reduction.

**4. Calculate the Price Reduction:**
- Section 24(2) states the reduction must be an 'appropriate amount'. This is typically calculated as the difference between the contract price and the value of the goods in their faulty state.
- **Formula:** Price Reduction = Contract Price - Value of Faulty Goods
- **Calculation:**
  - Contract Price: £500
  - Value of Faulty Goods: £150
  - Price Reduction = £500 - £150 = £350

**Conclusion:** Ben is entitled to a price reduction. An appropriate amount would be £350. The trader must refund this sum to Ben, and Ben keeps the washing machine.

learningObjectives: Outline implied terms under CRA 2015 for goods and services.
Explain satisfactory quality, fitness for purpose, as described.
Analyse consumer remedies: repair, replacement, refund.
Compare CRA with Sale of Goods Act for business contracts.

faq.q: If I buy a cheap item and it breaks quickly, can I still claim it wasn't of 'satisfactory quality'?
faq.a: Yes, potentially. While s.9(2) states that price is a key factor in determining what is 'satisfactory', it does not eliminate the right. A very cheap item is not expected to have the same durability or finish as an expensive one, but it must still be functional for its common purpose for a reasonable period. For example, a £5 kettle should still boil water safely, even if it lacks premium features. The key is what a reasonable person would expect at that price point. If it breaks after two uses, it is likely not of satisfactory quality.

faq.q: Does the CRA 2015 apply if I buy something from a private individual on an online marketplace?
faq.a: No. The CRA 2015 only applies to contracts between a 'trader' (a business) and a 'consumer' (an individual). A private sale between two individuals is a consumer-to-consumer (C2C) transaction and is not covered by the Act. In such cases, the principle of *caveat emptor* ('let the buyer beware') largely applies. The buyer's rights are very limited, usually only that the seller had the legal right to sell the goods and that the goods match their description (under the Sale of Goods Act 1979). The key protections for quality and fitness under the CRA 2015 do not apply.

faq.q: What's the difference between s.9 (satisfactory quality) and s.10 (fitness for a particular purpose)?
faq.a: Section 9 concerns the general, objective quality of the goods for all their common purposes. For example, a waterproof coat should be waterproof under normal conditions. Section 10 is more specific and applies when a consumer has a particular, perhaps unusual, purpose in mind and communicates this to the trader, relying on their expertise. For example, if a consumer asks a trader for a coat suitable for a specific polar expedition, they are invoking s.10. The coat must then be fit for that *specific* purpose, which is a potentially higher standard than just being a normal waterproof coat for everyday use.

flashcards.back: Business-to-**consumer** contracts for goods, services, and digital content — replaces much of SGA 1979 for consumers.
Goods must meet standard a reasonable person would regard as satisfactory — considering description, price, and other circumstances.
Goods must be fit for purpose consumer makes known to trader — express or implied.
Service performed with **reasonable care and skill**; if price/time not agreed, reasonable price within reasonable time.
Consumer may reject within **30 days** if goods do not conform — full refund (s20–22).
After 30 days or if consumer chooses — trader must repair or replace; if impossible, price reduction or final right to reject.

flashcards.front: What is the standard for 'satisfactory quality' under s.9 CRA 2015?
flashcards.back: The standard of what a reasonable person would regard as satisfactory, considering the price, description, and all other relevant circumstances. Key factors include fitness for common purposes, appearance, safety, and durability.

flashcards.front: When does the implied term of 'fitness for a particular purpose' (s.10 CRA 2015) apply?
flashcards.back: It applies when the consumer makes a specific purpose known to the trader (expressly or by implication) and relies on the trader's skill or judgement to provide suitable goods for that purpose.

A 'consumer' is an individual acting for purposes wholly or mainly outside their trade, business, craft, or profession. A 'trader' is a person acting for purposes relating to their trade, business, craft, or profession.

flashcards.front: What does s.11 CRA 2015 require regarding the description of goods?
flashcards.back: It implies a term that where goods are supplied by description, the goods must correspond with that description. This is a strict liability term, meaning any deviation is a breach.

flashcards.front: What are the three key implied terms for digital content under the CRA 2015?
flashcards.back: Section 34 (to be of satisfactory quality), Section 35 (to be fit for a particular purpose), and Section 36 (to be as described).

flashcards.front: What are the 'first-tier' remedies for faulty goods under s.23 CRA 2015?
flashcards.back: The right to one repair or one replacement of the non-conforming goods. This is the default remedy after the 30-day short-term right to reject has passed.

flashcards.front: When can a consumer access 'second-tier' remedies under s.24 CRA 2015?
flashcards.back: After one failed repair/replacement, or if repair/replacement is impossible or not done in a reasonable time. The remedies are a price reduction or the final right to reject.

flashcards.front: What is the 'final right to reject'?
flashcards.back: A right to a full refund for non-conforming goods, available under s.24. If exercised after 6 months, the trader can make a deduction from the refund to account for the use the consumer has had of the goods.

comparisonTable.caption: Comparison of Implied Terms: CRA 2015 vs. SGA 1979

cells: Business-to-Consumer (B2C) contracts made on or after 1 Oct 2015.
Business-to-Business (B2B) contracts and all consumer contracts made before 1 Oct 2015.
Specific statutory rights and remedies for digital content (ss.34-47).
No specific provisions. Treated as 'goods' only if on a tangible medium (e.g., a CD).
Clear tiered system: Short-term right to reject (30 days), right to repair/replacement, then a final right to reject or a price reduction.
Right to reject for breach of condition, but this right is lost if the buyer is deemed to have 'accepted' the goods. Damages are a primary remedy.
A term seeking to exclude or limit liability for ss.9, 10, and 11 is not binding on the consumer (s.31).
Can be excluded in B2B contracts if the exclusion clause is 'reasonable' under the Unfair Contract Terms Act 1977.
